content.full_text:
November 1, 1976


Nancy S. Bradshaw
4949 Kendall Street
San Diego, California 92109

Dear Ms. Bradshaw:

     Thank you for your letter of September 27, 1976, concerning
the discretion of criminal prosecutors to enforce criminal
provisions of the Political Reform Act. I apologize for the
delay in responding.

     As you know, under Government Code Section 91001(a), the
"Attorney General is responsible for enforcing the criminal
provisions of this title with respect to state agencies, lobbyists
and state elections. The district attorney of any county in which
a violation occurs has concurrent powers and responsibilities
with the Attorney General." The Fair Political Practices
Commission has no responsibility for enforcing the criminal pro-
visions of the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, we are by a
copy of this letter referring your letter to the Attorney General's
Office for response.

     Your letter can not be considered an opinion request
under Government Code Section 83114. Government Code Section
83114 states:

     Any person may request the
     Commission to issue an opinion
     with respect to his duties
     under this title....[Emphasis added]

     Pursuant to this statutory authorization, regulations adopted
by the Commission require that requests for opinions be sub-
mitted by the person whose duties are in question or by that
person's representative. Your letter does not state that your
duties are in question nor does it identify the person on behalf
of whom you have submitted the opinion request. For these reasons,
no formal opinion will be issued by the Commission.

          We appreciate your interest in the Political Reform Act
     and urge you to bring to our attention other suspected violations
     of it. If you have any questions, please contact Barbara Campbell,
     an attorney in the Enforcement Division.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Frederic Hanelt
                                        Director
                                        Enforcement Division

     FH:MA

     cc:  John Gordnier
          Office of the Attorney General
          555 Capitol Mall
          Sacramento, CA

     √bcc; Jeanne Pritchard

Sep 28 11 41 AM '76

4949 Kendall Street
San Diego, California, 92109
September 27, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California, 95814

Dear Sirs:

We would like you to render an opinion regarding the interpretation of Government Code, Section 91001(a) as it relates to Government Code, Section 91002.

We would like your opinion regarding the obligation of the criminal prosecutors to enforce the criminal provisions of Title 9 criminal prosecutors to enforce the criminal provisions of Title 9 prior to pending elections in cases in which the investigation of alleged violations of Title 9 have been completed by the Criminal Prosecutors before said elections.

There is a large group of people in San Diego who would like to have the legislative intent of the People when they enacted Title 9 interpreted NOW!

It appears that the provisions of Government Code, Section 91002 in disqualifying a person convicted of a misdemeanor under made 9 from being a candidate for an elective office for a period of four years, indicates that any criminal prosecutions under Title 9 should be conducted in a timely and speedy manner, including and especially 9 should be conducted in a timely and speedy manner, including and especially before a pending election, if possible.

It also appears that the provisions of Government Code, Section 91002 would have no meaning and would be moot after an election if the offender were a candidate in such an election because,

1. If the offender lost the election, he would not longer be a "candidate," so he would no longer be subject to the punishment described in said section. Besides, he may even not be interested in being a candidate for public office again.

2. If the offender won the election, he would still be able to take office. The only punishment that would be meted out would be that he could not run for re-election.

It seems that Government Code, Section 81001(a), (b), (h), and (i) and Government Code, Section 91002 was Section 81002 (a) indicate that Government Code, Section 91002 was intended to be used for officiating elections and not former candidates and officials-elect.

Although Government Code, Section 91001 (a) gives Criminal Prosecutors the authority to enforce the criminal provisions of Title 9, and Government Code, Section 91000 (c) sets forth the statute of

- 1 -


limitations for commencement of such criminal proceedings, Title 9
appears to be completely silent on the issue of whether enforcement
of the criminal provisions should be undertaken before a pending
election whenever possible.

It appears from the foregoing, however, that the legislative
intent was to have enforcement proceedings commenced prior to
pending elections whenever possible, especially in view of the
provisions of Government Code, Section 3121, which debies the
urgency of having matters resolved before a pending election.

What we want your opinion to tell us is whether Title 9 can be
construed to give the Criminal Prosecutor discretion to wait until
after a pending election to enforce the criminal provisions of
Title 9 or whether such construction would violate the legislative
intent of Title 9.

We shall look forward to hearing from you.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]

NANCY S. BRADSHAW, Ph.D.,
for herself and behalf of
unnamed electors in the
State of California


- 2 -

Bradshaw 4949 Kendall Street San Diego, California 92109 Dear Ms. Bradshaw: Thank you for your letter of September 27, 1976, concerning the discretion of criminal prosecutors to enforce criminal provisions of the Political Reform Act. I apologize for the delay in responding. As you know, under Government Code Section 91001(a), the "Attorney General is responsible for enforcing the criminal provisions of this title with respect to state agencies, lobbyists and state elections. The district attorney of any county in which a violation occurs has concurrent powers and responsibilities with the Attorney General." The Fair Political Practices Commission has no responsibility for enforcing the criminal provisions of the Political Reform Act. Accordingly, we are by a copy of this letter referring your letter to the Attorney General's Office for response. Your letter can not be considered an opinion request under Government Code Section 83114. Government Code Section 83114 states: Any person may request the Commission to issue an opinion with respect to his duties under this title....[Emphasis added] Pursuant to this statutory authorization, regulations adopted by the Commission require that requests for opinions be submitted by the person whose duties are in question or by that person's representative. Your letter does not state that your duties are in question nor does it identify the person on behalf of whom you have submitted the opinion request. For these reasons, no formal opinion will be issued by the Commission.
